bins.name: Preserve State Interconnection Authority
bins.desc: The filers support FERC jurisdiction over interstate transmission and wholesale markets but strongly oppose any preemption of state authority over end-use customer interconnection and local distribution facilities. They cite Order 888, the FPA's explicit reservations, Loper Bright, and the Major Questions Doctrine as reasons a broad jurisdictional assertion would face serious legal risk.
quotes: The Joint Consumer Advocates support jurisdictional federal avenues for regulating the interstate transmission and wholesale market aspects of large load interconnection, while respecting states' existing authority, including the authority to interconnect large loads at the distribution level.
FERC has never claimed the kind of jurisdiction over end-use customer interconnection that the ANOPR proposes.
The ANOPR risks lengthy, burdensome, and meritorious legal challenges to the extent that it ignores the FPA's and FERC's explicit acknowledgements of state jurisdiction.

A FERC final rule that includes federal preemption of any existing, traditional state authority will face an uphill battle under the U.S. Supreme Court's new standard of review for agency statutory interpretation.

bins.name: 20 MW Aggregate Threshold
bins.desc: The filers endorse the ANOPR's 20 MW threshold and specify it should apply to the customer's aggregate power requirement across all points of interconnection to prevent splitting loads to avoid coverage.
quotes: The Joint Consumer Advocates support the ANOPR's minimum threshold of 20 megawatts (MW) for both large loads and hybrid facilities, and this threshold should apply to the customer's aggregate power requirement rather than to a single point of interconnection.

bins.name: No Shortcuts on Study Quality
bins.desc: The filers support expedited study processes for curtailable and dispatchable large loads but insist studies must fully comply with NERC guidelines and reliability standards. They oppose mandatory timelines for EDC studies given the complexity of large load types.
quotes: The interconnection study process should not be accelerated at the expense of reliability or accuracy of identifying network upgrades.
The interconnection study process should comply with all NERC guidelines and Reliability Standards and not be accelerated at the expense of reliability or accuracy of identifying network upgrades.

bins.name: Standardized Queue With Deposits
bins.desc: The filers support a centrally managed interconnection queue with standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties to deter speculative projects and improve demand forecasting accuracy.
quotes: The Joint Consumer Advocates support establishing a large load and hybrid interconnection queue that is centrally managed by the same entity that manages/administers resource adequacy processes, like PJM. This queue should have standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties to deter speculative projects and provide transmission providers and RTOs with useful information to forecast demand on their systems accurately.

bins.name: Full Cost Responsibility, No Credits
bins.desc: The filers endorse 100% cost responsibility for network upgrades assigned to large loads with no crediting mechanism, and advocate for a but-for causation test to ensure all upgrade costs driven by the large load are directly assigned rather than rolled into network rates.
quotes: The Joint Consumer Advocates agree that load and hybrid facilities should be responsible for 100% of the network upgrades assigned to them through the interconnection studies and that these costs should not be offset through a crediting mechanism. Such a mechanism risks other ratepayers paying for the network upgrades that are the responsibility of the load and hybrid facilities.

The Joint Consumer Advocates fully support revisiting a 'but-for' test when determining which costs should be paid 100% by the large load customer, as opposed to a general benefits test, because but for the large load, the transmission owner would not have incurred the costs to upgrade the network facilities to serve that customer and the upgrades would not have been put into service without the interconnection of the large load addition.

bins.name: Ratepayer Protections Package
bins.desc: The filers urge FERC to require minimum contract terms, an 85% minimum demand charge, demand ramping requirements, and financial collateral from large loads so that stranded transmission costs do not fall on other ratepayers if a large load reduces load or exits.
quotes: The Joint Consumer Advocates support a package of ratepayer protections to the extent that the Commission finds them to be jurisdictional: 1) minimum contractual duration, renewal, and cancellation provisions; 2) minimum demand payments and demand ramping requirements; 3) financial collateral and guarantees.

The Joint Consumer Advocates support the establishment of clear contract terms regarding duration, renewal, and termination fees. Additionally, a minimum demand charge of 85% of forecasted peak demand at the conclusion of the ramp period would be reasonable.

bins.name: Comprehensive, NERC-Compliant Studies
bins.desc: The filers support studying large loads and hybrid facilities together with generation to minimize upgrade costs, but insist the process be technically sound, comply with all NERC guidelines, and not sacrifice reliability or accuracy for speed.
quotes: The interconnection study process should not be accelerated at the expense of reliability or accuracy of identifying network upgrades.

The Joint Consumer Advocates support establishing a large load and hybrid interconnection queue that is centrally managed by the same entity that manages/administers resource adequacy processes, like PJM. This queue should have standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties to deter speculative projects and provide transmission providers and RTOs with useful information to forecast demand on their systems accurately.

The Joint Consumer Advocates support large loads and hybrid facilities that interconnect to the system only if they bring sufficient capacity (either through generation and reserves or capacity-backed demand response) to offset the additional load brought onto the grid.
The interconnection study process should comply with all NERC guidelines and Reliability Standards and not be accelerated at the expense of reliability or accuracy of identifying network upgrades.

bins.name: Cost Causation for All Grid Costs
bins.desc: The filers call for strict cost causation throughout the rulemaking: large loads must pay 100% of upgrade costs without credits, face an 85% minimum demand charge, and provide financial collateral so no costs are shifted to residential ratepayers.
quotes: The Joint Consumer Advocates seek to ensure that these ratepayers do not pay higher rates for less reliable (i.e., inferior) service resulting from the interconnection of large loads that do not offset their load through the addition of timely, local, and adequate capacity resources.

The Joint Consumer Advocates agree that load and hybrid facilities should be responsible for 100% of the network upgrades assigned to them through the interconnection studies and that these costs should not be offset through a crediting mechanism. Such a mechanism risks other ratepayers paying for the network upgrades that are the responsibility of the load and hybrid facilities.

The Joint Consumer Advocates fully support revisiting a 'but-for' test when determining which costs should be paid 100% by the large load customer, as opposed to a general benefits test, because but for the large load, the transmission owner would not have incurred the costs to upgrade the network facilities to serve that customer and the upgrades would not have been put into service without the interconnection of the large load addition.

The Joint Consumer Advocates support a package of ratepayer protections to the extent that the Commission finds them to be jurisdictional: 1) minimum contractual duration, renewal, and cancellation provisions; 2) minimum demand payments and demand ramping requirements; 3) financial collateral and guarantees.

The Joint Consumer Advocates support the establishment of clear contract terms regarding duration, renewal, and termination fees. Additionally, a minimum demand charge of 85% of forecasted peak demand at the conclusion of the ramp period would be reasonable.

bins.name: Capacity Offset Required for Co-location
bins.desc: The filers support co-located and hybrid facilities but condition interconnection approval on the large load bringing sufficient offsetting capacity to avoid worsening resource adequacy in the PJM region.
quotes: The Joint Consumer Advocates support large loads and hybrid facilities that interconnect to the system only if they bring sufficient capacity (either through generation and reserves or capacity-backed demand response) to offset the additional load brought onto the grid.

bins.name: PJM Resource Adequacy Crisis Context
bins.desc: The filers situate their comments in PJM's current resource adequacy and affordability crisis, where 30 GW of projected peak load growth through 2030 comes from data centers and capacity prices have risen more than tenfold, underscoring the urgency of protective requirements.
quotes: The Joint Consumer Advocates support establishing a large load and hybrid interconnection queue that is centrally managed by the same entity that manages/administers resource adequacy processes, like PJM. This queue should have standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties to deter speculative projects and provide transmission providers and RTOs with useful information to forecast demand on their systems accurately.

bins.name: Protect Residential Ratepayers
bins.desc: The overarching theme of the filing is protecting residential electricity consumers in Pennsylvania and Delaware from paying higher rates or receiving less reliable service because of large load interconnection. The filers propose a multi-layer protection package covering cost assignment, minimum charges, contract terms, and financial collateral.
quotes: The Joint Consumer Advocates seek to ensure that these ratepayers do not pay higher rates for less reliable (i.e., inferior) service resulting from the interconnection of large loads that do not offset their load through the addition of timely, local, and adequate capacity resources.

The Joint Consumer Advocates support a package of ratepayer protections to the extent that the Commission finds them to be jurisdictional: 1) minimum contractual duration, renewal, and cancellation provisions; 2) minimum demand payments and demand ramping requirements; 3) financial collateral and guarantees.

The Joint Consumer Advocates support the establishment of clear contract terms regarding duration, renewal, and termination fees. Additionally, a minimum demand charge of 85% of forecasted peak demand at the conclusion of the ramp period would be reasonable.

bins.name: Jurisdictional Overreach Invites Litigation
bins.desc: The filers warn that an overbroad NOPR asserting federal jurisdiction over end-use customer interconnection will face meritorious legal challenges from states, particularly given Loper Bright's elimination of Chevron deference and the possible applicability of the Major Questions Doctrine.
quotes: The ANOPR risks lengthy, burdensome, and meritorious legal challenges to the extent that it ignores the FPA's and FERC's explicit acknowledgements of state jurisdiction.

A FERC final rule that includes federal preemption of any existing, traditional state authority will face an uphill battle under the U.S. Supreme Court's new standard of review for agency statutory interpretation.

bins.name: Large Load Capacity Offset Condition
bins.desc: The filers support interconnecting large loads and hybrid facilities only when they bring or procure sufficient capacity to offset their addition to grid load, whether through on-site generation, reserves, or capacity-backed demand response.
quotes: The Joint Consumer Advocates support large loads and hybrid facilities that interconnect to the system only if they bring sufficient capacity (either through generation and reserves or capacity-backed demand response) to offset the additional load brought onto the grid.

bins.name: Full NERC Standard Compliance
bins.desc: The filers support requiring all utilities and PJM to abide by NERC Reliability Standards and guidelines for large load interconnection, and ask that any deviations be disclosed to stakeholders with an explanation.
quotes: The interconnection study process should comply with all NERC guidelines and Reliability Standards and not be accelerated at the expense of reliability or accuracy of identifying network upgrades.
